description.s: Hob is a small, interpreted Lisp dialect designed specifically for Hobson. It combines Lisp's implementation model — s-expressions, an interpreter, macros, homoiconicity — with Smalltalk's interaction philosophy: live inspection, graphical tools, and the principle that the tool and the material are made of the same stuff.
The language has three distinguishing properties:
Item references are a primitive type.
Reading an item is a language-level operation, not an API call. The runtime tracks dependencies automatically.
The AST is serialized into the item store, making code queryable, navigable, and modifiable with the same tools used for data.
The interpreter's eval loop is async JavaScript internally, but Hob code is written and experienced as synchronous. The concept of promises does not exist in the language.

The end state is a Hobson where the kernel, all views, all libraries, and all user code are written in Hob, with the interpreter itself being the only JavaScript item. The development experience aspires to Smalltalk: a live browser where you navigate items, see their structure, click through references, modify code, and watch changes propagate instantly.
First-class reference to an item in the store
Item references can use either a name (
). The runtime resolves names to IDs at evaluation time. An unresolvable reference is an error.
Linked list; when evaluated, first element is called as function
Indexed array; evaluates to itself (no function call semantics)
Vectors are used for Hiccup-style DOM descriptions:
Maps are used for item content, function options, and general data.
These are handled directly by the evaluator, not as function calls.
— Bind a name in the current module's scope.
— Create an anonymous function. Supports destructuring.

(fn [x y] (+ x y))
(fn [{:keys [title body]}] (str title ": " body))
— Local bindings. Supports destructuring. Independent bindings (those that don't reference earlier bindings in the same
) are evaluated concurrently — see Concurrency section.
— Conditional. Two or three branches. Only
— Sequence of expressions; returns the last.

(loop [i 0 sum 0]
  (if (> i 10)
    sum
    (recur (+ i 1) (+ sum i))))
— Parallel let. All bindings are evaluated concurrently. Bindings must be independent (no binding may reference another binding in the same
— Access a field on an item or map.

(. @my-note :title)           ; => "Shopping List"
(. @my-note :type)            ; => @note-type (item reference)
(. @my-note :children)        ; => vector of child specs
(. some-map :key)             ; works on plain maps too
(.. @my-note :type :name)     ; fetch note, get its type (a ref), fetch that, get name
register dependencies with the reactivity tracker when evaluated inside a reactive context (e.g., during view rendering).
Hob is mostly functional, but persistent items and atoms require explicit mutation.
— Save an item to the store. The
suffix is a convention for side effects. The storage backend is implicit — the interpreter knows which store to use.
— Read an atom's value. No shorthand — the
sigil is reserved exclusively for item references.
— Update an atom by applying a function.

(swap! counter inc)     ; counter is now 1
(swap! counter + 5)     ; counter is now 6
— Set an atom to a new value.

;; Named function
(def add (fn [a b] (+ a b)))

;; Shorthand
(defn add [a b] (+ a b))

;; Multi-arity
(defn greet
  ([name] (greet name "Hello"))
  ([name greeting] (str greeting ", " name "!")))

;; Variadic
(defn log-all [& args]
  (each args (fn [a] (log a))))

;; Anonymous shorthand (for simple cases)
#(+ %1 %2)       ; equivalent to (fn [a b] (+ a b))
Macros receive unevaluated syntax and return transformed syntax. They run at expansion time, before evaluation.

(defmacro when [test & body]
  `(if ~test (do ~@body) nil))

(defmacro unless [test & body]
  `(if ~test nil (do ~@body)))

;; Threading macro — pipes a value through forms
(defmacro -> [val & forms]
  (reduce (fn [acc form]
    (if (list? form)
      (concat [(first form) acc] (rest form))
      (list form acc)))
    val forms))

;; Usage:
(-> @my-note
    (. :content)
    (. :title)
    (str/upper-case))
;; Expands to: (str/upper-case (. (. @my-note :content) :title))
Each code item is a module. Modules export bindings explicitly.

;; In a library item named "string-utils"
(defn capitalize [s]
  (str (str/upper-case (str/first s)) (str/rest s)))

(defn truncate [s max-len]
  (if (<= (count s) max-len)
    s
    (str (subs s 0 (- max-len 3)) "...")))

(export capitalize truncate)

;; In another item
(require string-utils)
(string-utils/capitalize "hello")   ; => "Hello"

;; Or import specific bindings
(require [capitalize truncate] from string-utils)
(capitalize "hello")                ; => "Hello"
form loads a code item by name, evaluates it, and returns its exports.
module system regardless of what language the library or caller is written in. The dispatch is:
resolves the item by name, checks the cache
— evaluated as an ES module (blob URL +
— evaluated via a Hob interpreter instance, exports collected
In both cases, the result is a plain JS object of exports, cached by item ID. This works because Hob values
are JS functions, vectors are arrays, maps are objects. No marshaling is needed.
This means the language boundary is invisible to both sides:

;; Hob view requiring a JS library — works
(require doc-ast-renderer)

;; Hob view requiring a Hob library — also works, same path
(require string-utils)

;; JS view requiring a Hob library — also works
;; const utils = await api.require('string-utils');

The library's interpreter instance is created, runs, produces exports, and is discarded. The exported functions (closures over their Hob environment) survive as cached JS values. This is exactly how JS modules work too — evaluate once, cache the exports, discard the source context.

The eval loop is async JavaScript internally, so individual Hob expressions are naturally sequential. However, many Hobson operations (item fetches, queries) are I/O-bound, and running them sequentially is unnecessarily slow. Hob provides concurrency at two levels.
bindings for data dependencies. If a binding does not reference any name defined earlier in the same
block, it is independent and can be evaluated concurrently with other independent bindings. This is a compile-time analysis — the evaluator scans each binding's AST for references to earlier names.

;; These two bindings are independent → evaluated concurrently
(let [notes (find-items {:type @note-type})
      tags  (find-items {:type @tag-type})]
  ...)

;; 'y' depends on 'x' → evaluated sequentially
(let [x (get-item id)
      y (. x :title)]
  ...)
For cases where automatic analysis isn't sufficient.

;; plet — all bindings evaluated concurrently (no dependency analysis, all must be independent)
(plet [a (get-item id1)
       b (get-item id2)
       c (get-item id3)]
  (process a b c))

;; pmap — like map but evaluates the function on all elements concurrently
(pmap get-item ids)
(pmap (fn [note] (. note :title)) notes)
internally. They are built-in forms, not macros.

The async/await evaluator has a fundamental limitation: CPU-intensive Hob computations (large maps, complex view trees) block the JavaScript thread until the next I/O await point, freezing the UI. A continuation-passing style (CPS) evaluator would solve this by making every evaluation step suspendable. The interpreter could yield after N steps, check for pending user input, and resume — like React's fiber architecture. CPS also enables cooperative concurrency (interleaving multiple computations), cancellation, and time-budgeted evaluation. This is the right long-term architecture for a Smalltalk-inspired live environment, but adds roughly 2x implementation complexity compared to async/await. Defer until there's concrete evidence of UI responsiveness problems (likely Phase 4+).
Views return vectors describing DOM structure. The interpreter's runtime translates these to real DOM.
First element is a keyword (tag name):
Optional second element is a map (attributes)
Remaining elements are children (strings, numbers, or nested vectors)
A view is a code item that exports a

(def-view @note-type [item]
  (let [{:keys [title body tags]} (:content item)]
    [:div.note-view
      [:h2 (or title "Untitled")]
      [:div.note-body (render-doc-ast body)]
      (when (seq tags)
        [:div.note-tags
          (for [tag-ref tags]
            (let [tag (resolve tag-ref)]
              [:span.tag (. tag :name)]))])]))
is a macro that expands to:
Creating a view item with the render function as its code
Creating a view-spec item that binds the view to the target type
fetches an item reference and registers a reactive dependency. When any resolved item changes, the view re-renders.
expands to creating a library item with
metadata and the handler function in
, exactly as the current declarative watch system works. Hob macros generate the same item structures — they just make them easier to author.

;; Simple filter query
(find-items {:type-extends @note-type
             :where (fn [item] (contains? (. item :tags) @project-tag))})

;; Datalog-style (future, requires query engine)
(query
  [?item :type-extends @note-type]
  [?item :content.tags ?tag]
  [?tag :name "project"])
The initial implementation would use the simple filter form, which maps directly to
+ filter. The Datalog-style syntax is a future aspiration that would require building a query engine — likely Phase 7 or beyond.

;; Call a method on a JS object
(.method js-object arg1 arg2)

;; Access a JS property
(.-property js-object)

;; Set a JS property
(set! (.-property js-object) value)

;; Create a JS object
(js-obj :key1 val1 :key2 val2)

;; Import a JavaScript library item
(require-js "item-search-lib")   ; loads as JS, returns JS module object
The FFI is intentionally minimal. Most Hob code should never need it — the standard library and DOM runtime handle the common cases.
The interpreter is a single JavaScript library item. It exports a function that the bootloader (or module system) calls to evaluate Hob source code.
Recursive descent parser. Input: string. Output: nested arrays/objects representing the AST.
Whitespace and comma skipping (commas are whitespace)
Walks the AST before evaluation. When it encounters a list whose head is a known macro name, it calls the macro function with the unevaluated arguments and replaces the form with the returned AST.
Expansion is recursive — the result is re-expanded until no macros remain.
Core macros defined in the standard library (in Hob):

(defmacro defn [name & decls] ...)
(defmacro when [test & body] ...)
(defmacro when-let [binding & body] ...)
(defmacro cond [& clauses] ...)
(defmacro -> [x & forms] ...)
(defmacro ->> [x & forms] ...)
(defmacro for [bindings body] ...)
(defmacro def-view [type params & body] ...)
(defmacro def-watch [name filter params & body] ...)
The core async eval loop. This is the heart of the interpreter.
in the loop is what makes async invisible. Every function call that might touch IndexedDB or other async APIs goes through this loop, and promises are automatically awaited before the result is used.
analyzes bindings for data dependencies before evaluating:
Lexical scoping with closures. Each environment is a frame with a pointer to its parent.
Implemented in JavaScript, registered in the global environment.
: Handled by the DOM runtime, not as explicit functions. The Hiccup vectors are the interface.
Translates Hiccup vectors into real DOM elements. Handles mounting, event handler wiring, and cleanup.
: Takes a Hiccup vector and a DOM container. Creates real DOM elements and inserts them.

: When a view re-renders (due to reactive dependency change), the runtime does a full replace of the view's DOM subtree. This is the simplest correct approach and avoids the complexity of building a custom virtual DOM diffing algorithm. A future optimization is to integrate a lightweight DOM diffing library (e.g., morphdom) for surgical patches — but this is deferred until benchmarks show it's needed.
, etc., the runtime attaches real DOM event listeners that invoke the Hob function. Since Hob functions are closures managed by the interpreter, the runtime wraps them:
: When a DOM subtree is removed (parent re-render replaces it), the runtime cleans up event listeners and removes the instance from the render registry.
event fires, the registry looks up all instances displaying that item and schedules batched re-renders.
declares which slice of data the view cares about. The selector output is compared via
on each item change — re-renders fire only when the selected data actually changes.
: No selector support — always re-render on own-item change.
: A working Lisp in the Hobson REPL. Can evaluate expressions, define functions, manipulate data.
Core standard library (arithmetic, strings, collections, predicates)
Error messages with source location (line, column) — do not defer this
REPL integration: a way to type Hob into the existing REPL and see results
). The REPL detects Hob code (e.g., by a prefix command like
or by detecting parentheses) and routes to the interpreter instead of JavaScript eval.
: Macros, DOM, reactivity, views, concurrency. Those build on this foundation.

:hob (let [notes (filter (fn [i] (= (:type i) "...note-type-id...")) (get-all))]
       (map (fn [n] (get-in n [:content :title])) notes))
;; => ("Shopping List" "Meeting Notes" "Project Ideas")
Phase 1 tests use the existing test-suite infrastructure (
. Four test suites cover the four components:
Tests the reader (text → AST) in isolation. No evaluator needed.
Whitespace: commas treated as whitespace, leading/trailing whitespace, multiple spaces
ignored, comment at end of expression
Source locations: every AST node carries line and column
Tests the evaluator and special forms. Uses the reader to construct input ASTs.
Self-evaluating: numbers, strings, booleans, nil, keywords all return themselves
Symbol lookup: defined symbol resolves; undefined symbol → error with name and location
: binds value, returns the value, accessible in subsequent expressions
: creates callable, correct arity, closure captures lexical environment
: basic bindings, sequential (later bindings see earlier ones), body returns last expr
: true branch, false branch, nil is falsy,
are truthy, two-branch (no else) returns nil
: evaluates all, returns last, single expression, empty do
: sum 1..10, fibonacci, recur with wrong arity → error
Function application: simple call, nested calls, higher-order (map), closures (counter factory)
Async transparency: function returning a Promise → result is auto-awaited, no Promise leaks into Hob land
Error messages: undefined symbol, wrong arity, calling non-function — all include source location
Tests built-in standard library functions via
. Each test evaluates a Hob expression string and asserts the result.
Tests the interpreter's integration with Hobson's storage layer. Requires
retrieves a previously-created item by ID
with type filter returns matching subset
Unresolvable item reference → error with the reference name
function accepts a string and returns a value
: Phase 1 is complete when all four test suites pass (target ≥100 tests, 0 failures) and the manual REPL milestone works.
Phase 2: Macros, Concurrency, and Extended Standard Library
: The language becomes extensible and concurrent. Users can define macros. Parallel let and pmap make I/O-heavy code fast by default.
Quasiquote / unquote / unquote-splicing in the reader and evaluator
: Extend the interpreter item. The standard macros are defined in a separate Hob library item (
) that the interpreter loads on initialization.
: ~600 lines added to interpreter JS, ~300 lines of Hob stdlib.

:hob (defmacro unless [test & body]
       `(if ~test nil (do ~@body)))

:hob (-> @my-note
         (. :content)
         (:title)
         (str/upper-case))
;; => "SHOPPING LIST"

;; Concurrency test — these two fetches run in parallel:
:hob (let [notes (find-items {:type @note-type})
           tags  (find-items {:type @tag-type})]
       [(count notes) (count tags)])
Phase 2 tests extend the test infrastructure established in Phase 1. Five new test suites cover the new language features. As with Phase 1, each test item is CODE type, tagged with the test tag (
Add macro expander, quasiquote reader, destructuring, atoms, plet/pmap, try/catch, auto-parallel let, anonymous fn shorthand
, quasiquote reader syntax, macro expansion, and the standard macros written in Hob.
defines a macro callable by name
x` is AST, not a value
Macro expansion is recursive: macro that expands to another macro call
Error in macro expansion: body throws → error with macro name and source location
bindings — both sequential (vector) and associative (map) destructuring.
(loop [[x & xs] [1 2 3] acc 0] (if (nil? x) acc (recur xs (+ acc x))))
(loop [{:keys [n acc]} {:n 5 :acc 1}] (if (<= n 1) acc (recur {:n (dec n) :acc (* acc n)})))
Error on destructuring non-sequential as vector:
. These tests verify correctness and that independent bindings actually run concurrently. The concurrency suite is highest priority — subtle bugs here cause intermittent failures downstream.
Auto-parallel timing: two slow operations (using atoms + timeouts to measure) run in less time than sequential
Dependency analysis: binding referencing outer scope (not earlier let) is independent:
(def x 1) (let [a x b x] (+ a b))
all concurrent: verify all bindings in plet run in parallel (timing test with atoms)
error: if one binding throws, plet reports the error
with slow ops completes faster than sequential
preserves order: results match input order even when operations complete out of order
analysis: verify the dependency analyzer correctly identifies free symbols in nested expressions (internal function test)
(let [a (atom 0) b (do (swap! a inc) 1) c (do (swap! a inc) 2)] (deref a))
(def a (atom 0)) (swap! a inc) (swap! a inc) (swap! a inc) (deref a)
error handling, including nested handlers and rethrowing.
No error: try body returns normally:
Rethrow: inner catch rethrows to outer:
(def a (atom 0)) (try (throw "err") (catch e nil) (finally (swap! a inc))) (deref a)
— behavior depends on implementation (null vs error)
(loop [i 0 acc []] (if (>= i 3) acc (recur (inc i) (conj acc (try (if (= i 1) (throw "skip") i) (catch e nil))))))
Uncaught error propagates: verify that an error not caught by try/catch propagates to the caller

The macro and concurrency suites are highest priority — subtle bugs here cause intermittent failures and hard-to-diagnose issues downstream.

: The DOM runtime is added to the interpreter item. A small adapter layer (could be a separate item or part of kernel-rendering-system) bridges the kernel's view lookup with the Hob interpreter.
The adapter detects whether a view item contains
(Hob source) and routes evaluation accordingly. This means Hob views and JavaScript views coexist transparently.

Diffing is intentionally deferred. Full subtree replacement is correct and simple. If benchmarking reveals performance issues in complex views, integrate morphdom (a lightweight DOM morphing library that can be loaded as a JS library item) as an optimization pass. Building a custom virtual DOM is not planned.

: Phase 3 is complete when both new test suites pass (~45 tests, 0 failures) alongside all Phase 1 and Phase 2 suites (237 tests, 0 failures). The DOM runtime suite is highest priority — Hiccup rendering bugs cause broken views that are hard to debug. The view integration suite validates the kernel bridge which is the most architecturally sensitive piece.
Phase 4: Watches and Events in Hob
: The declarative watch system can be authored in Hob. Event-driven code (error handling, lifecycle hooks, search indexing) works in Hob.

Watcher for parent event type receives child events: watch on
Watcher for specific event type only receives that type: watch on
No filters (only event type) → matches any event of that type
Type filter → only matches items of exact type
TypeExtends filter → matches items whose type chain includes the given type
ID filter → only matches specific item
Combined filters → all must match (AND semantics)
Filter with no item in event content → filter fails (returns false), handler not called
array to the item, trigger an event → the Hob handler is called (verify via side effect: atom update, item modification, or log capture)
Hob watcher coexists with JS watcher: both fire for the same event
Watcher with multiple watches: first matching watch triggers handler, second does not cause double-fire
Watcher for unknown event type: no error, handler simply never fires
Empty watches array: no error, no dispatch

Phase 5 tests cover the dependency tracker, tracking context management, automatic re-renders, reactive atoms, and integration with the kernel event system. One new test suite.

Tests the dependency tracker, tracking contexts, automatic re-render triggering, reactive atoms, and cleanup. Unit tests exercise the tracker directly; integration tests verify the rendering pipeline re-renders on item changes.
→ registry lookup → selector check → batched re-render
inside a tracking context records the ID as a dependency
outside a tracking context still works (returns the item, no tracking side effect)
calls with different IDs in one tracking context records all of them
with the same ID in one context records it only once (set semantics)
inside a tracking context registers the atom as a dependency
outside tracking does not trigger re-render (no active context)
triggers re-render of contexts that deref'd the atom
Atom dependency is distinct from item dependency (atoms are local, items are storage-backed)
Re-render clears old deps before re-tracking (stale deps from previous render removed)
Context cleanup on unmount removes all subscriptions (no dangling references)
New deps added on re-render replace old deps (item A was accessed in render 1 but not render 2 → A no longer a dependency)
Render a Hob view that calls
, verify the view re-renders with new data
Render a view that accesses item A and item B, update only A → view re-renders; update only B → view re-renders
Render a view that accesses item A, update unrelated item C → view does NOT re-render
Re-render updates the dependency set: view initially accesses A, after re-render now accesses B instead → updating A no longer triggers re-render, updating B does
event for a tracked item triggers the re-render callback
event for a tracked item triggers the re-render callback (or cleanup)
event does not trigger re-renders (new items can't be in any dep set)
Multiple views depending on the same item: updating that item re-renders all of them
Rapid successive updates coalesce into a single re-render (debounce/microtask batching)
on a tracked item does not cause infinite re-render loop
Cycle detection breaks the loop after a configurable max depth (e.g., 3 re-renders)
Warning is logged when a cycle is detected
View that accesses no items has empty dep set, never auto-re-renders
with async values still tracked correctly
: The Smalltalk-inspired experience. A live item browser, structural inspection, and code navigation — all written in Hob.
This is where the Smalltalk philosophy materializes. The tools are themselves Hob items, inspectable and modifiable.

A view for any item that shows its full structure: fields, type chain, children, content. Each item reference is a clickable link. Type chain is displayed visually. Content fields are editable inline.

(def-view @atom [item]  ;; atom is the root type, so this is the fallback inspector
  [:div.inspector
    [:div.inspector-header
      [:span.inspector-type 
        (for [t (type-chain item)]
          [:a {:on-click #(navigate! t)} (. t :name)])]
      [:h2 (or (. item :name) (. item :id))]]
    [:div.inspector-fields
      (for [[k v] (. item :content)]
        [:div.field
          [:label (name k)]
          (render-field k v item)])]])

A Smalltalk-style browser for navigating code items by type. Left panel lists types that have code items (views, libraries, watches). Selecting a type shows its associated code items. Selecting a code item shows its source in an editor. Changes are live.
This is a container view with panels, written entirely in Hob. It uses
For any view item, shows which items it depends on (from the dependency tracker). For any data item, shows which views depend on it. Rendered as a graph or list of links.
An interactive view where you type a
expression and see results live. Results update reactively as items change. Saved queries are themselves items.
Syntax highlighting for Hob (a CodeMirror mode or custom highlighter)
Auto-completion for item names and standard library functions
Result display that renders item references as clickable links
: ~1500–2000 lines of Hob across multiple view and library items.

: Navigate to any item and see the inspector. Click on its type to navigate to the type item. Click on a view reference to see the view's source. Edit the view's source and see the original item re-render. The system explains itself.
(inspector renders for arbitrary item types, code browser navigates type → code items, dependency viewer shows tracked deps). Primarily integration/UI tests using
All Hob tests use the existing Hobson test infrastructure:
for execution in isolated nested instances, and the
type for UI and result persistence. Tests are code items tagged with
API. This lets test suites exercise the reader and evaluator in isolation without needing the REPL or DOM.
: Each test evaluates a Hob expression string and asserts the result. Reader tests call
directly and inspect AST nodes. Evaluator and stdlib tests call
and assert return values. Integration tests use
to create items and verify the interpreter interacts with storage correctly.
: When a bug is found, a test is added to the relevant suite before fixing it. The test runner shows all suites in one view, making regressions visible immediately.
: Each phase defines a minimum test count and zero-failure requirement. A phase is not complete until its tests pass alongside all previous phases' tests.
The eventual aspiration is a Hobson where the kernel modules are rewritten in Hob, with the interpreter itself being the only JavaScript item. The system would be fully self-describing.
It should only be attempted once Phases 1–6 are stable and battle-tested. It's high-risk because it changes the foundation of the running system.
: One module at a time, in order of least to most critical.
— Trivially simple, just CSS injection. Good first test.
— Relatively isolated, only active in safe mode.
— Moderately complex but well-contained. The Hob REPL replaces the JavaScript REPL.
— The event bus. Simple data structure but foundational.
— The most conceptually interesting: the module system, written in Hob, loads Hob modules via the interpreter. Requires careful design to avoid circularity.
— The most complex module. View lookup, instance registry, partial re-render.
— Validation, type chain checking. Straightforward translation but critical.
— The orchestrator. Depends on all other modules. Rewritten last.
: The bootloader needs a small modification. Currently it loads
as a JavaScript ES module via blob URL. After this, it would load the
JavaScript item, then use the interpreter to evaluate

: If the kernel-in-Hob has a bug that prevents boot, the system is bricked. Mitigation: Safe mode must be robust. Consider keeping a JavaScript-based safe mode in the interpreter item that bypasses the Hob kernel entirely. Additionally, the existing export/import mechanism means users always have a JSON backup they can load into a fresh instance.
: ~2100 lines across standard library, development tools, and example views
: ~8–12 weeks of focused work for Phases 1–6, with useful milestones at every phase.
sigil is reserved for item references (
). There is no shorthand for atom deref — use
. This eliminates all ambiguity in the reader:
followed by any token is always an item reference. Item references are the more common and more Hobson-native operation, so they get the prime syntax.
The storage backend is implicit in the interpreter's environment.
. The interpreter knows which store to use. This keeps the language clean — storage is an implementation detail, not a concept Hob users should think about.

are available for explicit control. This covers the main concurrency need (parallel item fetches) without introducing async concepts into the language. A CPS-based evaluator is the right long-term architecture for UI responsiveness during heavy computation, but is deferred until concrete benchmarks justify the complexity.
No custom virtual DOM — full replacement now, morphdom later

Phase 3 uses full DOM subtree replacement on re-render. Building a custom virtual DOM diffing algorithm is not planned. If performance becomes an issue, morphdom (a lightweight DOM morphing library) can be integrated as a JS library item. This keeps the initial DOM runtime small (~400 lines) and avoids months of edge-case debugging.
is a macro that expands to
. It always returns a vector, which is the right type for Hiccup children. No lazy sequences — simplicity over generality.
JS arrays for vectors, plain objects for maps

Hob vectors are JavaScript arrays. Hob maps are plain JavaScript objects. This makes FFI zero-cost (no marshaling) at the expense of Hob data being mutable from JavaScript. Acceptable tradeoff for a single-user system.

Watches (Phase 4) are simpler than reactivity (Phase 5) and deliver visible value immediately (logging, indexing, event handling). Reactivity requires careful engineering around dependency tracking and cleanup. Building watches first lets views work with explicit event handling, providing a working baseline before adding automatic reactivity.

Every Hob expression goes through the JavaScript eval loop. If this is too slow for complex views with many children, rendering will feel sluggish. Mitigation: benchmark early (Phase 3), optimize hot paths, and keep JavaScript as an escape hatch for performance-critical code. The DOM runtime in particular should be optimized JavaScript within the interpreter, not interpreted Hob.

When a Hob expression fails, the JavaScript stack trace points into the interpreter's eval function, not at the Hob source. Users need Hob-level stack traces with source locations. This requires the interpreter to maintain a Hob call stack and format it on error. Not hard, but easy to deprioritize and painful if neglected. Must be implemented in Phase 1, not deferred.

Naive macros can accidentally capture variable names from the calling context. This leads to subtle bugs that are hard to diagnose. Mitigation: use gensym (generated unique symbols) in macro definitions, and document the issue clearly. Full hygienic macros (like Scheme's
) are more complex and probably not worth the implementation cost initially.

A view that reads an item, then triggers a write (via an event handler that runs synchronously), could cause an infinite re-render loop. Mitigation: the dependency tracker should detect cycles (same view re-rendering more than N times in a single event cascade) and break with an error.

Passing values between Hob and JavaScript requires marshaling. Using JavaScript arrays for vectors and plain objects for maps makes FFI zero-cost but means Hob data structures are mutable from JavaScript. Accept this tradeoff — Hobson is a single-user system where this is unlikely to cause problems.

If a core form turns out to be wrong (e.g., the scoping rules, the destructuring syntax, the item reference semantics), it's painful to change after code has been written. Mitigation: keep the language small. Every feature should be justified by a concrete Hobson use case. Phase 1 and 2 are cheap to iterate on; don't add features speculatively.

Hob is a new language to learn. If the development tools (Phase 6) are weak, users may prefer to keep writing JavaScript. Mitigation: make Phase 6 excellent. The browser, inspector, and query explorer should make Hob feel more productive than JavaScript for Hobson-specific tasks.
No phase depends on future phases for usefulness. Phase 1 is a better REPL. Phase 3 is a better way to write views. Each is worth doing even if you stop there.
Hob supplements JavaScript; it doesn't replace it by force. Any item can use JavaScript. Migration is gradual and voluntary. There is never a moment where existing JavaScript code stops working.

The entire Hob implementation is a single library item (plus the Hob stdlib as a second item). It doesn't require special kernel support, additional bootloader complexity, or external dependencies. It's loaded via
. It participates in the same type system, same view lookup, same module system. The kernel's only awareness of Hob is a small adapter that says "if this item has
, evaluate it with the interpreter instead of as JavaScript."

Every phase is developed inside the running Hobson system. The interpreter is written in the Hobson REPL. Hob views are tested against live items. The development tools are built using the tools they replace. This is the Smalltalk way.
